notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Here’s what your data shows and what that means for your card setup right now.

From March–April 2026 in Monarch:

You’ve got one open credit card linked: “Chase Sapphire Preferred 8600.”
Your tracked spending on that card is basically:
Rent: $2,350 in March, $2,350 in April
Travel: $231.10 in April
No groceries, dining, gas, or other categories show up in that 2‑month window.
Monarch doesn’t have your card’s rewards structure or annual fee info.

Because of that, I can’t:

See your exact rewards rates (like 2x travel, 3x dining) or your annual fee.
Give a precise “this category → this card” plan beyond the fact that only one card is in Monarch.

Here’s how to think about your strategy with that in mind:

Right now, your setup is “one card for everything”

Every tracked purchase is effectively on Chase Sapphire Preferred 8600.
So your current strategy, at least in Monarch’s data, is just using that one card across the board.

Where you could earn more (conceptually)
Since I can’t see your actual rewards table, here’s the typical playbook you can line up against your cards:

Rent

Many landlords either don’t take cards or charge around a 2.5–3% fee.
You only come out ahead if your rewards value beats that fee, which is rare with standard 1–2% cards.
If you’re paying rent with a card and a fee, you’re likely losing money vs ACH/check unless you’re chasing a big sign‑up bonus.

Travel

If your card has a travel multiplier (like 2–5x on travel), that’s usually best for flights, hotels, and transit.
If you also have a flat‑rate card (like 2% on everything) and your travel card only gives 1x on some bookings, the flat‑rate card might actually be better there.

Other categories (even if they’re just not in this 2‑month slice)

Groceries: Use the card with the highest grocery multiplier.
Dining: Use your best dining/restaurant card.
Gas: Use your best gas card.
Online shopping / everything else: Use your highest flat‑rate card (for example, 2% back) when nothing is in a bonus category.

How to turn this into a concrete plan in 5–10 minutes

Since Monarch can’t see your rewards tables, here’s the quickest way to get to a real, tailored strategy:

List your cards and rewards (even ones not linked yet):

Card A: issuer + name, rewards by category, annual fee
Card B: same
Card C: same

Rough out your monthly spend by category:

Rent: $____
Groceries: $____
Dining: $____
Gas/Transit: $____
Travel: $____
Online shopping / other: $____

Then Monarch can do the math for you:

Map “Category → Best card → Approx rewards rate → Estimated extra rewards if you shift spend.”
Call out any card that isn’t earning enough to justify its annual fee.
Boil it down to a simple rule like:
“Use Card X for groceries & gas, Card Y for dining & travel, Card Z for everything else.”

If you’re up for it, send:

A quick list of all your credit cards with rewards and annual fees, and
Rough monthly spend by category (rent, groceries, dining, gas, travel, other),

and Monarch will build a specific, card‑by‑card strategy and show you exactly where you can earn more.
